flashcards.front: What problem does Peterson's solution address?
flashcards.back: The critical section problem for two processes, ensuring mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.

flashcards.front: Name the two shared variables in Peterson's algorithm.
flashcards.back: 1. flag[2] (boolean array) 2. turn (integer)
Process i wants to enter its critical section.

flashcards.front: Purpose of 'turn' variable in Peterson's solution?
flashcards.back: Determines priority when both processes want to enter critical section simultaneously.

flashcards.front: Entry section pseudocode for Process i
How does Peterson's ensure mutual exclusion?
flashcards.back: Both processes can't pass while loop simultaneously due to turn variable and flag checks.

flashcards.front: What is busy-waiting in Peterson's context?
flashcards.back: Process spins in while loop until condition changes (consumes CPU cycles).

flashcards.front: Three requirements satisfied by Peterson's solution
flashcards.back: 1. Mutual Exclusion 2. Progress 3. Bounded Waiting
Busy-waiting (wastes CPU resources) and works only for two processes.
flag[i] = false;

flashcards.front: Why is turn variable needed when both set flag?
flashcards.back: Breaks symmetry by giving priority to one process when both attempt entry.
Process gets next turn after other process's critical section (max 1 wait cycle).
Used in simple embedded systems and academic contexts (not modern OS due to limitations).
Peterson's uses busy-waiting (software solution), semaphores use blocking (OS support).
Assumes load/store operations for flag and turn are atomic (not interrupted).
No, designed only for two processes. Use Bakery algorithm for N processes.
Process not in CS must let others proceed via turn variable and flag checks.
Turn variable ensures one process always yields (no infinite postponement).
Pure software solution (no hardware support needed).

flashcards.front: Why not used in modern OS?
flashcards.back: Busy-waiting inefficient for multitasking, limited to two processes.
Process resets flag[i] to false, allowing others to enter.
Turn variable decides which process enters first (last to set turn wins).
while (flag[j] && turn == j)
No, but turn variable ensures fair access if both compete repeatedly.
1. Mutual Exclusion 2. Progress 3. Bounded Waiting
